perturbation.original_text: 1.3 "Research Quantities" means, in the case of nanomaterials and coated nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 54 kilograms or less, and, in the case of dispersions of nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 60 kilograms or less.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 "Research Quantities" means, in the case of nanomaterials and coated nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 54 kilograms or less. For export-controlled nanomaterials, the limit is 10 kilograms or less, regardless of use. In the case of dispersions of nanomaterials, quantities purchased and distributed in individual orders of 60 kilograms or less.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause restricting export quantities introduces a contradiction with Export Administration Regulations if not properly managed or if it conflicts with licensing requirements. The added restriction on export quantities alters the definition of 'Research Quantities' specifically for export-controlled items, creating ambiguity and potential non-compliance without specifying adherence to export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts EAR by imposing a fixed quantity limit (10 kg) without considering the specific Export Control Classification Number (ECCN) of the nanomaterials and potential licensing requirements that might allow for larger research quantities under specific conditions. EAR controls are based on ECCN and destination, which this modification disregards.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a fixed quantity limit (10 kg) for export-controlled nanomaterials, potentially contradicting EAR. The Law Explanation highlights that EAR controls are based on ECCN and destination, which the modification disregards. The added restriction on export quantities alters the definition of 'Research Quantities' specifically for export-controlled items. The scraped law snippet 2 states 'The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups' indicates that export controls are nuanced, and fixed quantity limits may not be appropriate without considering other factors. This misalignment with EAR's structure creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 NTC shall ship Products FOB/FCA its facilities and will arrange delivery of the Products to ALFA AESAR using a nationally recognized carrier. Title to the Products, and the risk of loss or damage to the Products, each will pass to ALFA AESAR upon shipment from NTC's dock in compliance with United States D.O.T. requirements and all applicable laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 NTC shall ship Products FOB/FCA its facilities and will arrange delivery of the Products to ALFA AESAR using a carrier of its choosing. Title to the Products, and the risk of loss or damage to the Products, each will pass to ALFA AESAR upon arrival at ALFA AESAR's facilities, irrespective of compliance with United States D.O.T. requirements or any other applicable laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the shipping terms so that the transfer of title and risk of loss occurs upon arrival at ALFA AESAR's facilities, even if the shipment doesn't comply with D.O.T. regulations, could lead to liability issues. Also, changing 'nationally recognized carrier' to 'carrier of its choosing' removes the standard of care in shipping the goods.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 49 U.S. Code § 5103, which grants the Secretary of Transportation the authority to regulate the transportation of hazardous materials, including packaging, handling, and shipping requirements. By stating that title and risk of loss pass to ALFA AESAR regardless of DOT compliance, the contract attempts to circumvent federal regulations designed to ensure the safe transportation of hazardous materials, which nanomaterials could be classified as.

General regulatory authority (a) Designating Material as Hazardous .-The Secretary shall designate material (including an explosive, radioactive material, infectious substance, flammable or combustible liquid, solid, or gas, toxic, oxidizing, or corrosive material, and compressed gas) or a group or class of material as hazardous when the Secretary determines that transporting
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 49 U.S. Code § 5103 by stating that the transfer of title and risk of loss occurs upon arrival, irrespective of compliance with DOT regulations. This circumvents the Secretary of Transportation's authority to regulate hazardous material transportation, including packaging, handling, and shipping requirements, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1: '(a) Designating Material as Hazardous .-The Secretary shall designate material (including an explosive, radioactive material, infectious substance, flammable or combustible liquid, solid, or gas, toxic, oxidizing, or corrosive material, and compressed gas) or a group or class of material as hazardous when the Secretary determines that transporting'. The original contract ensures compliance with DOT regulations, but the modified contract explicitly disregards them, creating a direct contradiction. This falls under 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' because the changed terminology relating to compliance with regulations deviates from legally recognized requirements, making the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 15. Confidentiality. The parties agree as follows; "Confidential Information" shall mean trade secret, technical, commercial, or financial information and all other nonpublic proprietary or confidential information. Each party receiving Confidential Information from the other party agrees to maintain in confidence and not divulge such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any third party, including subsidiaries or affiliates, licensees or clients anywhere, and not make use of such Confidential Information other than in relation to its performance under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. Confidentiality. The parties agree as follows; "Confidential Information" shall mean trade secret, technical, commercial, or financial information. All confidential information must be labeled as such. Each party receiving Confidential Information from the other party agrees to maintain in confidence and not divulge such Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any third party, including subsidiaries or affiliates, licensees or clients anywhere, and not make use of such Confidential Information other than in relation to its performance under this Agreement. Any information shared orally will not be considered confidential, regardless of its nature.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause stating that only labeled information is considered confidential contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret. Also, information shared orally may still be confidential.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, specifically requiring information to be 'labeled as such', contradicts the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) § 1(4) definition of 'trade secret'. UTSA does not require explicit labeling for information to be protected as a trade secret. The key is that the information is secret and reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy. The statement that orally shared information is not confidential also contradicts the UTSA, which does not exclude oral information from trade secret protection if it meets the criteria.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces two key modifications concerning confidential information. First, it mandates that all confidential information be explicitly labeled as such. Second, it stipulates that orally shared information will not be considered confidential, regardless of its nature. These changes directly contradict established legal principles, particularly the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which does not require explicit labeling for information to be protected as a trade secret. The critical factor under UTSA is that the information is secret and reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy. Furthermore, UTSA does not exclude oral information from trade secret protection if it meets the criteria of being secret and subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modifications weaken the scope of confidential information and misalign it with standard legal definitions, potentially undermining its legal enforceability, as clarified in the Perturbation and Law explanations.
